frontmatter.title: 5. AI and images
frontmatter.subtitle: Diffusion models and the new picture-making

children.value: Image is the medium where Creative AI announced itself loudest. In 2022, when

arrived within a few months of each other, they changed picture-making faster than any tool since the smartphone camera. Designers, illustrators, journalists, lawyers, and the rest of us are still working out what that means.
This chapter introduces the technology underneath text-to-image models, the practical vocabulary of using them, and the editorial questions they raise.
. The idea is simpler than it looks.

children.alt: A simple diagram showing forward diffusion adding noise to an image step by step, and reverse diffusion removing it to recover an image.

children.value: Forward diffusion (top): start from a real image and progressively add noise. Reverse diffusion (bottom): start from pure noise and let a neural network remove it step by step, conditioned on a text prompt.
The training procedure has two halves:
Take a real image. Add a tiny bit of noise. Add a tiny bit more. Repeat many times until the image is pure static.
one step of noise at a time. Given a noisy image and how noisy it is, predict what was added.
Once trained, you can run the reverse half
: start with pure noise, denoise step by step, and a coherent image emerges. With
), you can steer that emergence — most commonly with a text prompt, embedded by a model like CLIP
The model can be reused for
version of an existing image. Less noise = more faithfulness to the input.
The model can be reused for
by only denoising the masked region.
The model can be reused for
by treating the canvas extension as a masked region.
trades quality for time. Many modern models can produce good results in 4–8 steps using distilled samplers.
adds a key efficiency trick: the diffusion does not happen in pixel space (millions of numbers per image) but in a compressed
space (thousands of numbers). This is why models can run on a consumer laptop.
The most recent generation of image models (e.g. SD3, FLUX) uses
When you use a text-to-image tool you will meet a small set of knobs.
want. Often more powerful than people expect.
— 1:1, 16:9, 9:16, 2:3, 3:2. Some compositions look fine in landscape and break in square.
— how strictly the model should obey the prompt. 5–9 is typical. Higher = more literal but oversaturated; lower = looser and more varied.
— number of denoising steps. 20–50 is typical for high quality; 4–8 for fast distilled models.
— the random seed for the noise. Same prompt + same seed = same image (within one model). Useful for iterating with a controlled variable.
— the algorithm that performs the denoising (Euler, DPM++, etc.). Affects style and convergence.
change one knob at a time
the CFG, you cannot tell what caused the change in output.
the same as language prompts. A working pattern is:

A wooden rowing boat moored at a fjord pier, viewed from a low angle,
black-and-white film photograph, soft early morning light, 50 mm,
nostalgic, in the style of late 20th-century Scandinavian photography
before you reach for style. “A car” is vague; “a 1972 Volvo Amazon parked in front of a yellow wooden house” is something the model can grip.
Material (“oil painting”, “pen drawing”), medium (“photograph”, “render”), and period (“1970s”, “Renaissance”) all do work.
“Photo-realistic illustration in watercolour” tells the model to choose between three things it cannot do at once.
, not on the whole prompt. If the lighting is off, change only the lighting words.
For models that support them, negative prompts are where you stop the failure modes you keep seeing:
blurry, extra fingers, watermark, low quality, deformed face
. Treat the negative prompt as a curated list, not a brain dump.
and stacks them freely; commercial products call them
This is where image generation stops being a slot machine and starts being a controllable creative tool.
A common, often more useful workflow is
: mask a region, replace it with something else. “Replace the bottle in his hand with a coffee cup.”
: extend the canvas. “Add another metre of beach to the left.”
specific objects with a single click.
You will get further with editing than with pure prompting for most professional jobs.
Hands, feet, text in pictures, jewellery, complicated logos.
— generally restricted by the major commercial tools.
“A man holds a cat with one hand and points at a sign that says ‘Open’ with the other” is at the edge of what current models can compose reliably.
Tools struggle to put accurate text into images. (Improving — but not solved.)
of an infographic but rarely produce accurate data.
Two pictures of “the same character” tend to drift. Solutions: character reference images, LoRA fine-tunes, image-to-image with seed locking.
with a clear subject, composition, and style.
Generate the image four times, each time changing
same prompt, same seed, four different aspect ratios;
same prompt, same seed, four different CFG values (3, 6, 9, 12);
prompt unchanged except for the lighting words.
Lay out the four grids in a single image (Canva, Figma, an empty markdown cell — anything). Caption each.
knob that mattered most for your subject. Write a short note on why.
Take a photograph (or screenshot) you have rights to.

If you have a Hugging Face account, the

import torch
from diffusers import StableDiffusionPipeline

pipe = StableDiffusionPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "stabilityai/sd-turbo",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
).to("cuda")  # or "mps" on Mac, or "cpu"

img = pipe("a wooden rowing boat at sunrise on a fjord, watercolour",
           num_inference_steps=4, guidance_scale=1.0).images[0]
img.save("boat.png")
Compare an AI-generated image of “a typical Norwegian street” with a real photograph. What is the model
What changes when image-making moves from “ten minutes for a sketch” to “ten seconds for a finished-looking picture”? Who benefits, who loses?
Pick a single image you generated this week. Write a 100-word caption that
describes how it was made — the tool, the prompt, the iterations, the edits.
